questions: What model am I talking to?

answer: RainBox is a local-LLM agent infrastructure: a small OS-process supervisor for AI agents, built from plain POSIX primitives without a workflow framework. A single main.py runs a Flask web app and an idle-by-default supervisor; each agent runs in its own child process, so a stuck or slow model can be killed without taking the whole system down. Work flows through a Postgres-backed inbox/journal queue, and agents are bound to model groups (priority-ordered fallback lists) served by local providers like Ollama and LM Studio, and cloud providers. Features include a group chat, a Q&A memory registry (where these answers come from), model configuration and benchmarking pages, a kanban board for agent task coordination, document-editing agents, and MCP. RainBox is Brain+Box minus the leading B.

What do you do when you don't know something?
answer: If no memory matches your question with enough confidence, I say I don't know rather than guess.

questions: How many things do you know?

answer: MCP is the **Model Context Protocol** — an open standard introduced by Anthropic in November 2024 for connecting AI assistants (LLM hosts) to external tools, data sources, and prompt templates in a uniform way. It's sometimes called "USB-C for AI tools" because it replaces the thicket of bespoke per-app integrations with one standardized connector: a single MCP-capable client can plug into any MCP server (filesystem, database, GitHub, browser, internal API, …) without rewriting glue code, and a tool author only ships the integration once. Concretely, an MCP server exposes three kinds of capabilities — tools (functions the model can call), resources (read-only data fetched by URI), and prompts (reusable prompt templates) — over JSON-RPC 2.0, either via stdio (local subprocess) or Streamable HTTP (remote). Hosts that already speak MCP include Claude, Codex, Cursor, and Zed; SDKs exist for Python, TypeScript, Java, Kotlin, C#, Go, Rust, and Swift. See: https://modelcontextprotocol.io

**slop** means low-effort, mass-produced AI-generated content: text, images, audio, or video made cheaply and published with little human review. It often looks plausible at first glance, but may be shallow, repetitive, misleading, or factually wrong. Common examples include spammy articles, fake reviews, clickbait images, auto-generated social posts, low-quality videos, and content made mainly to game search engines or recommendation feeds. Not all AI-assisted work is slop: the problem is volume-first publishing without care, editing, accountability, or useful intent. Treat suspiciously generic or viral AI-looking content with skepticism, and check reliable sources when accuracy matters. See: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AI_slop

Vibe coding means building software by describing the desired behavior to an AI coding tool, then iterating on the generated code through prompts, edits, tests, and review. It is common with tools such as Claude, Codex, Cursor. The term is often associated with fast, conversational development: explain the goal, let the model draft code, run it, inspect the result, then ask for fixes or improvements. Used well, vibe coding is a productivity technique. Used carelessly, vibe coding can become hard to troubleshoot: the code may work at first, but bugs, security issues, or later changes can be difficult to handle if the creator does not understand the system well enough. Non-programmers can still build successful projects with it, especially when they keep scope small, test often, and learn from the generated code. Good vibe coding still requires engineering judgment, source control, tests, security review, and knowing when to stop prompting and edit the code yourself. See: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vibe_coding

LM Studio is an app for running large language models locally on your own computer. It helps you discover, download, load, chat with, and serve local models. It has a graphical desktop interface, a local API server, and a command-line tool called lms for scripting and automation. The lms CLI can be used from the terminal for LM Studio workflows, such as managing models or starting local services. LM Studio is often used for private/offline AI experiments, testing open-weight models, and exposing a local OpenAI-compatible API for other tools. Compared with Ollama, LM Studio is more GUI-oriented, but lms also makes it useful from the terminal. See: https://lmstudio.ai and https://lmstudio.ai/docs/cli

Ollama is a tool for running LLMs locally, usually from the command line or through a local API. It can download models, manage them, and serve them to other programs. Developers often use Ollama when they want a simple local LLM backend for scripts, apps, agents, embeddings, or experiments. Compared with LM Studio, Ollama is more developer-oriented and automation-friendly. See: https://ollama.com

OpenRouter is an online service that provides one API for many different AI models from different providers. Instead of integrating separately with OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta, Mistral, and others, an app can call OpenRouter and choose among many hosted models through a single interface. It is useful for comparing models, switching providers, fallback routing, and managing cost/performance tradeoffs. Unlike LM Studio or Ollama, OpenRouter is cloud-based, so requests are sent to remote services. See: https://openrouter.ai

questions: LM Studio vs Ollama vs OpenRouter
difference between LM Studio Ollama and OpenRouter
answer: **LM Studio**, **Ollama**, and **OpenRouter** are different ways to run or access LLMs. **LM Studio** and **Ollama** run models locally on your own computer, so they need enough hardware: RAM, CPU, GPU, VRAM, disk space, and power. Local models can be private and work offline, but performance depends on your machine. **OpenRouter** uses cloud-hosted models from many providers through one API. Cloud models do not require powerful local hardware, but they usually cost money per token and send requests to remote services. In short: local LLMs trade hardware for control; cloud LLMs trade token cost for convenience and access to larger models. See: https://lmstudio.ai https://ollama.com https://openrouter.ai
